segments.text: BAM! Mr. Tarrou. In this lesson we are going to learn how to solve equations that have more
than one absolute value function in them. Our three examples we are going to work through
are all going to have two absolute value functions in them, but this technique works
for even more than two. There could be three or four absolute value functions in
a single equation and this technique will still work, even though that would be
a lot of work to go through. So the absolute value equations or graphs have turning
points that occur at values that make the expression inside the absolute values
equal to zero. You can rewrite the absolute value expressions as a piecewise
function. The absolute value of ax-b, the sort of general version of this
language that is on the right side of the board, has got sort of a linear function
inside this absolute value expression, but you could have more complex
expressions inside here. If we have ax-b, well then it is going to be rewritten as ax-b if x is greater than or equal to b over a.
And you are going to replace the absolute value of ax-b with a set of, the opposite expression, negative parentheses ax-b if x is less than b over a.
Well this b over a idea is again coming from, they are the turning points, they are coming from taking the
expression inside the absolute value equation or function and setting it equal to zero. And of course we are going to add by b and divide by a
with this sort of general form again expression that we are going to get x is equal to b over a.
This is the x value of the turning points. Okay. So what? Well for any equation involving absolute values there are at least
one turning point value for the variable, of the variable, for each absolute value. In other words again you might have more than just a linear expression
inside your absolute value function. So for each absolute value you may get more than one turning point.
The n turning points are used to split the number line into n plus one intervals. So if you find that you have two turning points,
two answers or x values that take the expressions inside your absolute value functions and making them equal to zero,
then those two turning points are going to cut your number line into three parts. Now within each of those three parts are those n plus one intervals.
Well within each interval each absolute value in your equation can be replaced by the expression inside the absolute value,
if what is inside that absolute value symbol is positive within that interval. If what is in that absolute value symbol is negative within this interval,
then you are going to replace it with its opposite or that set of negative parenthesis wrapped around the expression inside your absolute value function.
Now when you do that you are going to get an equation without any absolute value symbols in it.
Each of those new equations are going to be solved and when you do so you are going to be restricting your solutions to the corresponding interval
that you use to create that new equation. And if you have a graphing calculator or some way to graph these functions that you are trying to solve,
